risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To select the 42 health centers, all centers were assigned random numbers in Excel and sorted from smallest to largest; health centers were selected based on city zone and in order of their random number. Randomization was completed in STATA." Comment: The paper describes a specific random method for sequence generation using software-generated random numbers, with stratification by city zone. This is sufficient detail to support a truly random allocation sequence.

Quote: "42 public health clinics (first level of care facilities) were randomized to either the treatment or control conditions" and "Research assistants approached women in the waiting rooms of participating clinics, verified eligibility for the study..." and "Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic." Comment: This cluster trial recruited women after clinics had already been randomized, and the research assistants enrolling participants knew whether each clinic was assigned to treatment or control. Therefore, the upcoming assignment for each participant was foreseeable at the time of enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic." Quote: "Women who participated in the study at treatment clinics received the following intervention: (1) integrated IPV and health screening; (2) supportive care; (3) safety planning and harm reduction counseling; (4) supportive referrals; and (5) booster counseling sessions at three months post baseline." Quote: "Women in the control clinics were given a referral card containing general information on IPV and a list of resources..." Comment: The paper explicitly indicates lack of blinding of study personnel (research assistants), and the intervention and control conditions were clearly different, making participant blinding implausible. Outcomes were largely subjective/self-reported (e.g. past-year IPV, reproductive coercion, use of community resources, safety planning measures, quality of life), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence responses and care received. Therefore performance bias is high risk.

Quote: "Data were collected through a computer-assisted survey ... Given that clinics were randomized and not individuals, research assistants were not blinded to whether they were in a treatment or control clinic." Quote: "Our primary outcome is past-year IPV [32] and our secondary outcomes include past-year reproductive coercion [7, 23], use of community-based resources [33], safety planning measures [33], and quality of life [34]." Comment: Outcomes were primarily participant-reported via survey, so the patient is effectively the outcome assessor for key outcomes. These outcomes (IPV, reproductive coercion, safety planning, quality of life, resource use) are subjective/self-reported and could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. The study also explicitly states research assistants were not blinded. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective/self-reported outcomes makes this domain High risk.

Quote: "This total sample size takes into account an attrition rate of 45%." and "147 nurses are actively participating in the study (49% in treatment clinics) (26% staff dropped out primarily due to staff turnover at clinics)." Comment: This article is a study protocol/design paper and does not report analysed outcome data or per-arm numbers randomized vs analysed at the relevant follow-up time points for the outcomes of interest. The 45% figure is only an anticipated attrition rate used for sample-size planning, not observed outcome attrition. Although nurse dropout is mentioned, the paper does not provide clear outcome-specific analysed denominators by arm and time point, so attrition bias cannot be reliably judged as low or high.
